content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 21, 1990

Honorable Terry Holbert
City of Banning
1434 West Ramsey Street
Banning, California 92220

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-080

Dear Councilmember Holbert:

You have requested clarification of written advice previously
provided regarding the application of the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since your
request is for further clarification rather than for advice as to
a particular decision, we are treating your request as one for
informal assistance. (Regulation 18329(c).)²

QUESTION

When a councilmember is advised that he may not contact city
staff with respect to decisions which materially affect his
financial interest except for necessary contacts relating to the
processing of drawings and submissions, what does the term
"processing" mean?

CONCLUSION

The term "processing" would appear to cover the movement of
the drawings and submissions through the approval process.

___________________________

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-90-80
Page 2

Therefore, your contacts with agency staff with respect to draw-
ings and submissions of an architectural, engineering or similar
nature must be limited to either (1) contacts regarding matters
which are solely ministerial, secretarial, manual or clerical or
(2) responding to staff questions relating to the evaluation of
drawings and submissions prepared by you or relating to their
movement through the approval process.

FACTS

In September of 1989, we provided informal written assistance
to the city attorney of the city of Banning requested on behalf
of an unnamed councilmember. (McHugh Advice Letter, No. I-89-443,
copy enclosed.) You have now advised us that you were the
councilmember who requested the advice.

You would like clarification of language in the conclusion of
that letter. The statement in question is as follows:

     1. The councilmember may not contact city
staff to discuss or processing of development
projects, because sources of income to him are
directly involved in the decision. He may respond
to questions from city staff about the processing
or evaluation of drawings or submissions of an
architectural, engineering or similar nature.

McHugh Advice Letter, supra,
p.2, emphasis added.

You have requested clarification of the use of the word
"processing". You indicate that the use of the term seems to be
at odds with Section 18(b)(4) (copy enclosed) which provides
that an official may, in certain circumstances, make "necessary"
contact with agency staff concerning the processing or evaluation
of the drawings or submissions prepared by the official."

As indicated in our letter to Mr. McHugh, the Act prohibits a
public official from voting on, participating in, or using his or
her official position to influence a decision in which he or she
has a financial interest. (Section 87100; McHugh, supra, p.2.)
As a member of the city council, you are considered a public
official under the Act. (Section 82048; Regulation 18700, copy
enclosed.)

An official uses his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision in the following circumstances:

     With regard to a governmental decision which
is within or before an official's agency or an
agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of his or her agency, the official is
in control of his or her official position to
attempting to use his or her official position
influence the decision if, for the purpose of

Our File No. I-90-80
Page 3

influencing the decision, the official contacts, or
appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not
limited to, appearances or contacts by the official
on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer.

Regulation 18700.1(a), emphasis
added.

Therefore, the general rule enunciated by Regulation 18700.1
is that an official must not contact agency staff for the purpose
of influencing a decision in which he has a financial interest.
This includes contacts on behalf of a business entity, client or
customer.

Regulation 18700.1 contains an exception to the general rule,
which provides, in part, as follows:

(b)...[A]n official is not attempting to use
his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision of an agency...if the
official:

* * *

(4) Prepares drawings or submissions of an
architectural, engineering or similar nature to be
used by a client in connection with a proceeding
before any agency. However, this provision applies
only if the official has no other direct oral or
written contact with the agency with regard to the
client's proceeding before the agency except for
necessary contact with agency staff concerning the
processing or evaluation of the drawings or
submissions prepared by the official.

Emphasis added.

The exception set forth in subdivision (4) is narrowly
construed. In our letter to Mr. McHugh, we advised that "necessary contact" is limited to responding to questions from city
staff regarding drawings and submissions prepared by the official.
(McHugh, supra, p.7.) Otherwise, any contacts with staff would be
limited to actions which are solely ministerial, secretarial,
manual or clerical. (Regulation 18700(d)(1); Kennedy Advice Letter, No. I-86-026, copy enclosed.)

We have never specifically defined the term "processing" as
used in Regulation 18700.1(b)(4). The term would appear to cover
the movement of the drawings and submissions through the approval
process. Therefore, your contacts with agency staff with respect
to drawings and submissions of an architectural, engineering or
similar nature must be limited to either (1) contacts regarding

Our File No. I-90-80
Page 4

matters which are solely ministerial, secretarial, manual or
clerical or (2) responding to staff questions relating to the
evaluation of drawings and submissions prepared by you or relating
to their movement through the approval process.

I trust that this letter will provide you with additional
guidance in evaluating the appropriateness of particular contacts
with city staff. I have also enclosed the following advice letters which may provide additional examples of permissible and
impermissible contact:

Martello Advice Letter, No. A-85-190
Levinger Advice Letter, No. I-88-328
Finucane Advice Letter, No. A-88-189

If you have additional questions, or wish to discuss
particular applications, you may contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Margaret W. Ellison
    Counsel, Legal Division

KED:MWE:aa

Enclosures
